plainMeaning: Samādhi, Saṁvara, Yaśodhara, Vijaya, Malla and Deva, Anantavīrya and Bhadraṅkara — let us devotedly serve these Jinas who are yet to come.
recitation: samādhi susaṁvara, yaśodhara, vijaya mallīdeva. anantavīraja bhadrakṛta, tehnī kīje seva.

wordByWord.gloss: Samādhi — the 17th of the future twenty-four Jinas
wordByWord.etymology: Proper name from Sanskrit samādhi ('meditative absorption, equipoise'), sam + ā + √dhā ('to hold together').
wordByWord.notes: Opens the closing octet (Jinas 17–24) of the anāgata caubīsī.

wordByWord.gloss: the good Saṁvara — the 18th future Jina
wordByWord.etymology: Honorific su- ('good, auspicious') prefixed to the name Saṁvara (saṁ + √vṛ, 'stopping the inflow of karma').
wordByWord.notes: The su- is the poet's laudatory epithet; the Jina's name is simply Saṁvara.

wordByWord.etymology: Proper name, Sanskrit yaśas ('glory') + dhara ('bearer'), 'bearer of renown'.

wordByWord.gloss: Malla and Deva — the 21st and 22nd future Jinas
wordByWord.etymology: A metrical dvandva fusing two names: Malla (cf. Skt malla, 'wrestler/champion') and Deva (= Deva-jina, 'the divine Jina').
wordByWord.notes: Crucial reading: this single word names TWO Jinas, so the verse's seven written name-words still yield the eight Jinas 17–24.

wordByWord.etymology: Old-Gujarati/Apabhraṁśa form of Sanskrit ananta-vīrya ('infinite valour'), ananta ('endless') + vīrya ('energy, prowess'); -ja reflects the vernacular treatment of -ya.
wordByWord.notes: Spelling preserved verbatim per the book.

wordByWord.gloss: Bhadrakṛt (Bhadraṅkara) — the 24th and last future Jina
wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit bhadra ('auspicious, weal') + kṛt ('maker'), 'doer of good'; the canonical lists give Bhadraṅkara ('weal-bringer').
wordByWord.notes: Closes the future caubīsī. Name-form bhadrakṛt is the book's reading for Bhadraṅkara.

wordByWord.etymology: Old Gujarati genitive: demonstrative teha ('that, those', from Skt/Prakrit te) + the genitive suffix -nī.
wordByWord.notes: Refers back to all the named future Jinas.

wordByWord.gloss: let (it) be done; one should render
wordByWord.etymology: Old Gujarati passive/optative of karavũ ('to do'), from Sanskrit kriyate ('is done'), √kṛ.
From Sanskrit/Prakrit sevā ('attendance, devoted service'), √sev ('to serve, attend upon'); final -ā shortened for metre.
wordByWord.notes: Object of kīje; the affective climax of the name-list.

literalTranslation: Samādhi, the good Saṁvara, Yaśodhara, Vijaya, Malla(-and-)Deva, Anantavīrya, Bhadrakṛt — of these let service be rendered.
idiomaticTranslation: Samādhi and noble Saṁvara, Yaśodhara and Vijaya, Malla and Deva, Anantavīrya and Bhadraṅkara — to all of these let us offer our loving service.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: This verse seals the roll-call of the future caubīsī, naming its final eight awakened ones (the 17th through 24th). Reading it correctly turns on one compound: મલ્લીદેવ (mallīdeva) is not one Jina but two — Malla and Deva(-jina) — so the seven name-words deliver eight Jinas, exactly completing the set of twenty-four that the previous verse had carried up to Citragupta. Vācaka Mūlā compresses the names tightly for the metre and adorns only one, Saṁvara, with the auspicious su- ('good Saṁvara'), a poet's flourish rather than a separate title. What distinguishes this stanza from the bare lists before it is its final clause: તેહની કીજે સેવ — 'let their service be done.' Up to here the dhāla has simply been pronouncing names; now, on reaching the last of them, the poet converts naming into worship, issuing a gentle hortative that draws the reciter from memory into devotion. The grammatical impersonal (kīje, 'let it be done') makes the call universal — it is addressed to everyone reciting, in every dawn. The movement of the verse is therefore from enumeration to invitation: it gathers the remaining future Jinas and, in its last three words, tells us what to do with the whole list. The following verse then steps back to label them collectively 'the Jinas yet to come' and to close with the poet's bow, so this stanza is the hinge where the future caubīsī both ends and is first explicitly venerated.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The stanza enacts a distinctively Jain reach of reverence: it bows not to living or past teachers but to Jinas who have not yet been born. This is doctrinally sound because Tīrthaṅkara-hood is fixed in advance by the ripening of tīrthaṅkara-nāma-karma — the tradition already identifies the souls who will become these future Jinas (e.g. the soul of Śreṇika as the first, Padmanābha). To 'serve' (sevā) the not-yet-arisen is thus not fancy but an affirmation that the Jina-śāsana is beginningless and endless, a tīrtha renewed age after age. Rendering homage across all three times (past, present, future) is itself a strengthening of samyaktva (right faith): the worshipper's devotion is unbounded by the accident of when she happens to live.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: Reciting this verse, the practitioner deliberately extends bhakti beyond the familiar twenty-four of the present age to teachers who will only appear in a future cycle. The closing 'let service be done' is a daily reminder that veneration is an act to perform, not merely names to read — and it cultivates humility and patient aspiration: one serves a path far larger than one's own lifetime.

crossReferences.text: Enumeration of the anāgata caubīsī and the souls destined to become them

sources.title: Tirthankara
sources.consultedFor: Concept of the twenty-four Tīrthaṅkaras per age and the tīrthaṅkara-nāma-karma that pre-determines future Jinas.

sources.title: Bhadraṃkara, Bhadraṅkara — definitions
sources.consultedFor: Form and sense of the name Bhadraṅkara (bhadra + -kara, 'weal-maker') underlying the book's Bhadrakṛt.

sources.title: Sevā, Seva — definitions (Sanskrit/Prakrit/Jainism)
sources.consultedFor: Etymology and devotional sense of sevā ('attendance, devoted service', √sev) for the closing word સેવ.

sources.title: Anāgata — definitions
sources.consultedFor: Sense of anāgata ('not-yet-come, future') framing the anāgata caubīsī to which these eight Jinas belong.

notes: Key reading: મલ્લીદેવ (mallīdeva) is a metrical dvandva naming two distinct future Jinas — Malla (21st) and Deva/Deva-jina (22nd); only with this split do the seven written name-words complete Jinas 17–24, matching the canonical Samavāyāṅga sequence (Samādhi, Saṁvara, Yaśodhara, Vijaya, Malla, Deva-jina, Anantavīrya, Bhadraṅkara). 'સુસંવર' carries an honorific su- ('good') on the Jina-name Saṁvara, not a separate name. 'અનંતવીરજ' is the Old-Gujarati/Apabhraṁśa reflex of Skt ananta-vīrya, and 'ભદ્રકૃત' is the book's form of Bhadraṅkara; both spellings, plus the daṇḍa । and verse punctuation, are preserved verbatim per the source edition. Name-to-number assignments follow the standard Śvetāmbara list; minor spelling variants across editions do not affect identification.
